III 
 3D INTERNET 
 
A. 
WHY 3D INTERNET? 
 
The most  common heard arguments against the 3D Internet is in the form of the question ―why do we need it?‖ For many 
of its users the Internet is a familiar, sophisticated medium where we communicate with each other, get our news, shop, 
pay our bills, and perform various our day to day tasks. And it also Provides a new experience. We are indeed so much 
used to and dependend on its existence that we don‘t think about its nature anymore just like we do not think about 
Ohm‘s law when we turn on the lights. From this perspective what we have, i.e. we can say that 2D is more sufficient and 
the 3D Internet is yet another fad. But actually if we stop and think about the nature of the Internet for a moment we come 
to conclusion  that it is nothing but a virtual environment where various people and multiple organizations interact with 
each other and exchange the  information which they contain that is the reason 3D internet is needed. When we 
understand this  the question can be turned on its head and becomes ―why do we restrict ourselves to 2D pages and 
hyperlinks for all these activities? Instead of moving to advancements‖ Navigating hierarchical data structures is often 
cumbersome for large data sets. Unfortunately, the Internet as Within the website, at every level of the interaction, the 
developers have to provide the user  navigational help.  
Otherwise, theuser would get lost as soon as  or later. Since this is a very fast environment, there is no direct way of 
providing a navigation scheme which would be immediately recognizable to human beings. The situation is not any better 
when moving between websites. Given the current situation the term web surfing is rather appropriate as we don‘t have 
control over where the web takes us with the next click. Various experiments are done while developing 3D internet. 
Another problem is the emergence of search engines as a fundamental element of the Internet. It is no surprise that 
Google is the most powerful Internet company of our generation. There is actually a much better alternative way of 
arranging data which everybody knows and uses. We spendour lives in a 3D world navigating between places and 
arranging objects spatially.We rarely need search engines to find what we are looking for and our brains are naturally 
adept at remembering spatial relationships. 
We are living like 2D creatures living on flat documents not knowing where we are or what is next to us. We transfer 
constantly from one flat surface to another, each time getting lost, each time asking for directions or help.In contrast, the 
interest of use and intuitiveness of 3D GUIs are an immediate consequence of the way our brains work, a result of a long 
evolutionary process ensuring adaptation to our world. Although the 3D Internet is not solution to all problems, it 
provides an HCI framework that can reduce mental load and open doors to rich, innovative interface designs through 
spatial relationships. Another important point is the Web place metaphore of the 3D Internet which enables interaction 
between people in a natural way. In this sense, the 3D Internet can be seen as a natural successor of Web 2.0.3D internet a 
combination of graphics and internet. The metaverses such as SL can be considered as pioneering precursors of the 3D 
Internet. Yet, they already indicate its significant business opportunities. Not only existing online businesses would give 
from the inherent interactive nature and spatial HCI paradigms of the 3D Internet but also a whole range of businesses 
such as fashion, real estate, and tourism can finally start using the Internet connection effectively. We expect that the 
given of providing faithful 3D representations of products and services will have revolutionary effects on online business 
to business and business to customer commercial activity.

B.  
WHAT IS 3D INTERNET? 
We are in great honour in presenting and discussing a 3D Internet architecture as an illustrative example. It will be in 
normal use within few years. This shares the time-tested main principles and also as well as underlying architecture of the 
current Internet as well as many semantic web concepts these are used in development. Thus the various operational 
principles the 3D Internet shares with its predecessor including the open and flexible architecture and also the open 
protocols as well as simplicity at the network core even intelligence at the edges, and distributed implementation. The 
non-complex graphical depiction of the proposed 3D Internet architecture is provided in the diagram for better 
understand. We adopt here the terms universe, world, and web place as 3D counterparts of WWW, website, and sub 
domain, respectively. We describe each component‘s functionality briefly below: 
 
 
WORLD SERVERS: provide user- or server-side created, static and dynamic content making up the specific web 
place(3D environment) including visuals, physics engine, avatardata, media, and more to client programs. A world server 
has the important task of coordinating the co-existence of connected users, initiating communication between them, and 
ensuring in-world consistency in real time. They facilitate various services such as e-mail, instant messaging and more. 
Performance is increased by several times. 
 
 
 
 
AVATAR/ID SERVERS: virtual identity management systems containing identity and avatar information as well 
as inventory (not only in world graphics but also documents, pictures, e-mails, etc.) of registered users and providing 
these to individual world servers and relevant client programs (owner, owner‘s friends) while ensuring privacy and 
security of stored information. Avatar/ID servers can be part of world servers. 3D internet plays a vital role in avatar 
services 
 
 
UNIVERSE-LOCATION SERVERS: virtual location management systems similar to and including current 
DNS providing virtual geographical information as well as connection to the Internet via methods similar to SLurl. They  
also act as  distributed directory of the world, avatar servers and users. This is done possible by connecting location 
servers. 
 
 
CLIENTS: browser-like viewer programs running on users‘ computers with extensive networking, caching, and 
3D renderingcapabilities. Additional components of the 3D Internet include web places and 3D object 
creation/editingsoftware, i.e. easy-to-use 3D modeling and design programs such as Sketch-Up and standardized mark-up 
languages and communication protocols. Emergence of new software and tools in addition to the ones mentioned can be 
expected. Provides better user environment

IV  
WORKING 
 
A 
NETWORKING AND DISTRIBUTED COMPUTING 
 
The conventional approach of web caching will not be adequate for satisfying the needs of the 3D Internet environment 
consisting of 3D worlds, which may be hosted on different servers. One challenge arises from the fact that the avatars 
contain significantly more information about the user who is visiting a 3D world than cookies do about a 2D web site 
visitor. For instance, avatars contain information about appearance (e.g. height, clothing) and behavior (e.g. visible, open 
for conversation). As avatars tends to move between worlds, caching will be needed in server-to-server interactions to 
enable fast and responsive transition between worlds. This will be intensifiedwhen changing worldby avatars carrying 
objects (e.g. a bicycle) or virtual companions (e.g. a virtual dog) with them, whichrequires the transfer of large volumes 
of information in a short time. 
Another challenge is related to the fact that some virtual objects or companions are essentiallyrunning programs. They 
have snippet that defines how they react to certain inputs, and they have a partly autonomousbehavior. Thus, when an 
avatar moves to a world, the world server (or servers) needs to execute the corresponding code. This raises a number of 
research problems: how to safely run  untrusted code for instance, when the virtual companionsare user-generated and 
custom built? How will the economy of such transactions be handled? How to move running code between  world servers 
withoutfatally disrupting its execution? Platforms should allow the dynamic deployment of potentially 
untrustedcomputation at globally dispersed servers, in a fast, secure and accountable manner [6]. 
 
B. LATENCY MINIMIZATION 
 
As the 3D Internetincreases the reliability on graphics and interactivity, it will be crucial that the latency that clients 
notice while interacting with servers is minimized. It is known from existing implementations such as SL that high 
latency provides low responsiveness and reduced user satisfaction. Hence, the network has to be developed intelligentlyto 
overcome these challenges. We can use hybrid peer-to-peer (P2P) approach to reduce server load and ensure scalability of 
the 3D Internet infrastructure. It consists of three types of communications: client to server (C2S), server to server (S2S) 
and client to client (C2C) each with different latency and bandwidth requirements C2S communicationsbandwidth 
limited, frequently updated, andsynchronous. Location and  use of in-world services will spend substantial amount of 
resources for both client and world servers. The avatar/ID server-client C2S communicationsare less frequent and 
asynchronous. As an optimization, some portion of this communications can be moved to the backbone by facilitating 
S2S links between ID and world servers (solid gray lines) triggered by clients and through intelligent caching.

an unhindered and  seamless usage of services this could increase efficiency. The important main concept behind Single 
Sign On is federation is denoting the establishment of common references between accounts or identities in different 
repositories or even the services. The important Microsoft Passport1 and even several other systems that have been

been devised to allow authentication not based on user identities alone apart from that rather

A. 
INTELLIGENT ENVIRONMENTS 
Among the various emerging fields such as ubiquitous computing and ambient intelligence draw heavily from adaptive 
algorithms and intelligent algorithms they are useful in lot ways. They are concerned within the field of computing and 
networking technology which is unobtrusively embedded in the everyday environment of various  users. The various 
emphasis of the intelligent environments is on user-friendliness, efficient and also distributed services support,along with 
user empowerment, and also support for human interactions. All this assumes a dramatic and grand shift away from 
desktop or portable computers to a variety of devices accessible via intelligent interfaces this would result in new era. 
 
B.  
INTELLIGENT SERVICES 
In the scenario of  3D Internet, the concept of intelligent environments extends to underlying communication protocols 
naturally to a greater extend and enabling services as well as to user centered Services provide better performance. Given 
that it is its inherent P2P nature, it can make use of paradigms like the  intelligent routing where mechanisms being aware 
of the network topology along with the information structure that allow for flexible also context-dependent distribution of 
traffic . As in the real world, one could think of adaptive algorithms that control traffic flow depending on the time of day, 
user-behavior patterns, or a variety of global and local events. Since the 3D Internet provides an environment that closely 
resembles the physical world and can project the actual virtual world, it calls for intelligent interfaces that extend the 
conventional desktop metaphors such as menus and sliders. These includes various  speech- and gesture recognition, but 
this  also implies the various interactions with virtual objects and tools inspired by things existing in the real world. 
Learning and ambient intelligence on this level will then have to be concerned with typical usage patterns, anticipations 
of user activities, and convincing simulations. In case of the user-centered services, it is not tough to imagine applications 
of machine learning that would facilitate social interaction of users as well as increase usability of core functionalities 
along with better performances of the virtual environments on the 3D Internet. 
As per on the given user provided ratings or an analysis of typical usage of various patterns and even goal directed,  also 
intelligent searches [3] and various recommendations are possible to the next level. This of course facilitates 
personalization of individual users avatars  helps to identify the uniqueness

VI 
TECHNICAL IMPLICATIONS AND SOLUTIONS 
 
A. 
IMPLICATIONS 
 
SPEED: 
Internet speed is one of the most significant and also the most basic implications that are being faced by the 3D Internet in 
various parts of the world. A research that has conducted in many parts of the world clearly shows that not many 
countries in the world are in a state to fulfill the internet speeds that are required for the implementation of the 3D Internet 
this will create a backlog in 3D since high speed internet is very essential.  
 
HARDWARE: 
These kind of various  implications in the field of hardware are not quite serious implications to be thought of, because 
the main Hardware implication that we face to implement the 3D Internet is that the display device used to display the 
images are 2D in nature we have to convert the 2D into 3D this is the task, but with the inclusion of the 3D internet there 
would be great difficulty to view the 3D objects in the 2D devices and lot of concentration is needed in this domain. 
 
B. 
SOLUTIONS 
 
SPEED 
The currently widely used 3G is the third generation of tele standards and technology for mobile networking with 
increased capacity. These 3G networks are wide-area cellular telephone networks available provident that are evolved to 
incorporate high-speed Internet access and video telephony. We could possibly  expect that 3G will provide higher 
transmission rates: a minimum speed of 3Mbit/s and maximum of 15.4Mbit/s for stationary users, and 348  kbit/s in a 
moving vehicle without interruption. Hence, with the introduction of the 3G technology, the speed implications involved 
with the 3D Internet would be solved in the near future. 
 
 
HARDWARE 
 3D goggles is usage could be considered as the  solution that can be employed to overcome the problem of the Hardware 
implications.3D goggles available in the market in wide variety of ranges, we could be able to select from these wide 
variety of 3D goggles. The cost of these goggles is even not too much and comparatively low, so this prospect we could 
consider this in the preliminary stages of the 3D Internet,  these could be upgraded with the latest technologies in the 
upcoming years which could be used to implement/ display the 3D data. 
Use of Vision Station as a monitor / display for the 3D Internet, Vision Station is a computer display technology 
developed by Elumens that provides 180 degrees of viewing angle for its users. Current computer screens have at most a 
50-degree field of view and needs the user to move the controller in order to see the images that are not on the screen. 
This motion seems to be not natural because in the real world, users use their peripheral vision to see things beyond the 
direct line of sight. Thus kind of new display technology will address this limitation of standard computer 
monitorsinprove the quality.

3DXplorer V4 also extends support for user-generated content using commonly available design and modeling tools by 
adding the ability to import 3D data in the popular COLLADA format. The various  Modeling tools supporting 
COLLADA including the  Maya, the  3ds Max, the Light Wave 3D, Soft image |XSI; Houdini; MeshLab, SketchUp, 
Blender and also various others. Google Earth‘s native format which is COLLADA  and it‘s supported by Google 
SketchUp and the Google 3D Warehouse providing access to a huge amount of existing 3D models such as buildings, 
furniture, vehicles, etc which would be useful in further future developments. 
 
 VIII  FUTURE DVELOPMENT: 
3DXplorer V5 is a major release bringing unique, first-of-its-kind innovative features to the emerging web3D market. A 
very stronger and also more reliable cloud-based network architecture which has more choices and more categories of 
avatars, over a 100 avatars simultaneously in a single room that would be large enough, Enterprise edition enabling 
intranet installations also behind the firewall along with the unlimited number of rooms, white label services for sales & 
marketing partners and more possibilities of customizations are among the new features which would increase the 
better user experiance.

Switching from the existing version  to future V5, we‘ll start by a period of general availability of the new sophisticated

website, if you like it would be a great user experiance. 
 
A.  
EDUCATION 
3D Internet can be used as a great learning platform for education by many institutions, such as colleges, universities, 
libraries and government entities which provide more better learning experiances. 
 
 
E-LEARNING: 
Evaluation of the learning process is done by Computer Aided knowledge/skill 
Assessment system(CAA), which is a E-learning application. To improve the quality of distance and communication, 
technologies are applied in the education domain since they provide a way to perform experiment with equipment that is 
not physically present in the place, as the user. 
 
B.  
RELIGION 
Religious organizations can make use of the 3D Internet for multiple purposes just to open virtual meeting places within 
specified locations share their views. 
 
C.  
EMBASSIES 
We could create embassies in 3D Internet which can perform multiple functions, where visitors will be able to talk face-
to-face with a computer-generated ambassador about visas this would reduce time, trade and other issues and speedup the 
performances. 
 
D. 
LIVESPORT     
 
 
ENTERTAINMENT 
Various sports leagues like Cricket, Football, Professional Wrestling, boxing, Volleyball ,Atheletics, auto racing and 
many other games could be placed in the 3D Internet for it‘s users to play in the 3D environment providing an better 
experiance. 
 
E.  
ARTS 
We can  create new forms of art use the feature of modelling oin 3D internet, which in many ways are not possible in real 
life due to physical constraints or high associated costs and also in due to various reasons. Artists could display their 
works to an audience in and around their country with the help of 3D internet.  Many residents who buy or build homes 
can shop for artwork to place there in a more grand and attractive manner. Gallery openings even allow art patrons to 
"meet" and socialize with the artist responsible for the artwork and has even led to many real life sales. 3D internet could 
be used to enable live music performances 
 
IX  
CONCLUSION 
 
We are proud in presenting an overview of the concept 3D Internet and discussed the motivation behind it as well as the 
specific research directions in the fields of networking and security ,distributed computing also in machine learning and 
also in various other fields. Our belief is  that at this point in time we are facing a unique opportunity for the evolution of 
the Internet towards a much more versatile, interactive, and usable version of the 3D Internet which will be dominating 
the entire future of our society.

The emerging 3D applications along with the desktop paradigms also increasingly interactive nature of the Web 2.0, the 
Semantic Web efforts, with the widespread availability of powerful GPUs,novel input devices popularity, and changing 
intension in demographics of Internet users towards the younger, computer-literate generations, all provide the basis for 
the 3D Internet evolution. The hype surrounding metaverses (especially SL) should be seen under this light and taken as 
an indicator of the fact that many businesses are aware of the 3D Internet‘s potential the potential of 3D internet keeps 
growing to greater altitudes. 
However, to make the 3D Internet a reality it is necessary and important to start and continue multidisciplinary research. 
Here 3dXplorer will be a milestone for the emergence of the 3d internet in the future.

ANTI RAGGING COMMITTEE 
Functions & Responsibilities: 
 As ragging has many detrimental effects on students - Psychological, Social, Economical 
and Cultural aspects, which adversely impacts the standards of higher education, this 
team conducts interactivity sessions involving both Freshers and Seniors in order to 
create friendly and persuasive relationship among students. 
 The committee tries to create a safe and healthy environment among freshers. 
 The committee explains to the Senior students regarding the adverse effect of Ragging 
and punishments involved. 
 The committee displays anti-ragging notices in various locations of the campus and 
Contact Number of the committee members for immediate contact. 
 
ANTI RAGGING SQUAD COMMITTEE 
Functions & Responsibilities 
 To make rounds - all around the campus and other places vulnerable to incidents of, and 
having the potential of, ragging and to inspect such places and take precautionary 
measures / action to curtail any eventuality. 
 To make surprise raids on hostels, and other places vulnerable to incidents and having the 
potential for ragging to pre-empt any undesirable activity 
 To advise all students to treat equally other students of younger or elder classes and to 
help them in case of any need as a fellow human being. 
 To advise all students often to concentrate only on activities related to Academics and 
not on any other unrelated incidents/ events. 
 To advise students to be attentive all the time in order to avoid any unpleasant events 
inside the campus.

Agent Insurance Renewal 
 
1 S.Ajaykumar 2 D.Deepakkumar 3 S.Guna4 R.Vinolina 
1, 2, 3 Student, 4Assistant Professor,  
KGiSL Institute of Technology, Coimbatore 
 
ABSTRACT 
Agent Insurance Renewal app helps to reduce the work of the client and to keep them updated about the date 
of expiry and also the status of their policy. The agent can search any client with their respective Policy number or the 
Vehicle number registered with the policy. Having an insurance policy reduces the financial burden in the case of loss 
or damages arising from an accident caused to or by your insured vehicle. The renewal date of your insurance policy 
is based on the date your policy grabbed. Using this app, the agents can notify the clients through. E-mail or SMS 
regarding the unpaid (pending) amount and acknowledge for the renewal process. The agent can enter the type of 
vehicle for proceeding with the renewal process. The payment will be done to the insurance company by the client with 
the help of the agent. If the client forgets about renewing the policy, an alert will be sent by the application for the 
second time in order to notify the client again. The insurance can also be renewed by the client through this app.  
 
Keywords - policy-number, renewal, notification, payment, client details. 
 
I.  
INTRODUCTION 
 
The project “Agent Insurance Renewal” is about how the agent views all the policies of the clients that are 
registered with him. The agent can use the feature of notifying the client through an E-mail or a SMS about the policy 
expiry. The agent can select the type of vehicle and pursue with the process of renewal. The payment will be done by the 
agent and the client will pay the agent for the renewal of the policies. The calculation of the renewal amount will be in 
addition to the revised pattern of GST. Thein-built calculator calculates the desired amount and notify about the amount 
that has to be paid.  The alerts are prioritized with respect to the date of Renewal.  
 
II. 
VARIOUS TECHNOLOGY 
 
Kotlin is a statistically typed, general-purpose programming language with type inference. Kotlin mainly goals 
the JVM, but also accumulates to JavaScript or native code (via LLVM). Kotlin is officially supported by Google for 
mobile development on Android. Kotlin is comprised as an substitute to the standard Java compiler. The Android Kotlin 
compiler agreements the user choose between directing Java 6 or Java 8 companionable bytecode. Firebase is a mobile 
and web application development platform established by Google. Firebase Authentication is a facility that can 
substantiate users consuming only client-side code. It provisions social login benefactors Facebook, GitHub, Twitter and 
Google (and Google Play Games). Moreover, it contains a user management system whereby creators can enable user 
authentication with email and password login kept with Firebase. Firebase also delivers a Realtime database and backend 
as a service. The service provides application developers an API that allows application data to be synchronized across 
clients and stored on Firebase's cloud. The database is also open through a REST API and bindings for several JavaScript 
frameworks. Developers using the Realtime database can secure their data by using the company's server-side-enforced 
security rules.

III.  
EXISTING SYSTEM & PROPOSED SYSTEM 
 
The existing system of insurance renewal mostly are on webpages and apps. All the apps are third party 
company apps. These apps acts as a broker or an intermediate in insurance renewal. There is no support for E-mail or 
SMS notification. The existing application does not include insurance renewal calculation based on the current GST 
prices. The system is to be built with KOTLIN (a light-weight scripting technology) and Firebase for database. It 
calculates the renewal amount with respect to Government updates. Provide SMS and Email notification based on the 
expiry of the renewal date. Generate a renewal notice with Included taxes, no claim bonus and other commissions. The 
payment will be done by the client to the insurance company with the help of the agent.  
  
IV.  
DATA-FLOW DIAGRAM 
 
 
 
V.  
WORKING 
Agent Insurance Renewal comprises of insurance renewal of the agent, where entering the policy number of the 
client, the full details of the client will be displayed. The information consists of name of the client, policy details, vehicle 
details, date of renewal, issue date, inception date and expiry date.A notification mail or SMS will be sent to the agent 
before 30 days of expiry date. If the renewal is done, it generates renewal notice based on taxes and no claim bonus and 
agent commission.  If the expiry date of the renewal exceeds then again the notification will be sent. This application will 
be helpful for the agent to know their expiry date. The payment will be done by the client to the Insurance company 
through the agent.

VII.  
CONCLUSION 
 
The Agent Insurance Renewal helps the agent as well as the client to renew their policies on the go. They need 
not spend separate time in waiting in long queues for the renewal process. As the input data here is only the policy 
number, it will be easy for the agent to know about the policies that have been taken by the particular client. Here, If the 
client forgets to renew their policies, the renewal alert will be sent to the client for the second time to remind them

xxix
The main goal of this book is to assess the most recent trends in producing bionano-
materials from agricultural waste and microorganisms. The book title Agri-Waste and 
Microbes for Production of Sustainable Nanomaterials indicates that in 29 chapters 
this book has collected the knowledge, discoveries, and fruitful findings of green syn-
thesis of various nanomaterials using microorganisms and agricultural waste. Present 
book has been divided into two parts. Part I is focused on production of nanomateri-
als from agric-waste like metallic, copper, silica, cellulose, nanopolymers, and nano/
microplastics. Part II reviews appropriate biological methods such as agricultural and 
microbial synthesis of metallic/metal oxide, magnetic, silver, copper, nanomaterials, 
nano-ginseng, and nanonutrients, etc. Synthesis of nanocellulose from agri-wastes is 
a probable alternative for waste treatment methods and developing new biosensors 
and antimicrobial agents. Silicon nanoparticles have been predicted to become one 
of the professional ingredients for improving crop yields. With modern advances and 
extended work in enhancing nanomaterial synthesis performance and discovery of 
their biomedical, environmental, and agricultural applications, it is hoped that the ex-
ecution of these methods on a large scale and their industrial applications in different 
fields will take place in the near future. The use of agro-waste and microbes not only 
minimizes the cost of nanomaterial production but also decreases the need to apply 
dangerous chemicals; in addition, it encourages “green synthesis” and a strong step 
toward agricultural sustainable development. This book represents the fifth volume 
in the series titled “Nanobiotechnology for Plant Protection,” the original book series 
approved by Elsevier. The present volume contains 29 chapters written by eminent 
authors from Australia, Egypt, Bulgaria, Chile, Republic of Korea, Nigeria, South 
Africa, India, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, Pakistan, Turkey, and the United States. 
The 29 chapters have been written by professionals and experts with outstanding 
knowledge on the use of agri-waste and microbes for the production of green nano-
materials. This book follows a multidisciplinary approach and will be very useful for 
academics, teachers and scholars, agro-food environmental scientists working in ag-
rochemicals, nanotechnology, materials sciences, genetics, chemistry, physics, plant 
sciences, chemical processing, microbiology, plant physiology, biotechnology, and 
other stakeholders such as indus. This book follows a multidisciplinary approach and 
will be very useful for academics, teachers and scholars, agro-food environmental 
scientists working in agrochemicals, nanotechnology, materials sciences, genetics, 
chemistry, physics, plant sciences, chemical processing, microbiology, plant physi-
ology, biotechnology, and other stakeholders such as industrial waste management 
companies. Expected readers of this book would include scholars, graduate students, 
postgraduate students, and researchers in agrochemical firms and from diverse fields 
of science and technology. A study of this nature is important to start a database 
to formalize the practice of nanotechnology. Only very few institutions around the 
world ­specialize in agricultural and ­microbial ­nanotechnology. So we look forward 
to providing a positive, insightful, and innovative viewpoint not only to experienced

readers but also to ­technology decision makers with limited experience in this field

xxxi
The field application of engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) has not yet been well 
investigated for plant promotion and protection in an agro-environment, and many 
of the most effective components have been studied only theoretically or using pro-
totypes, which makes it hard to evaluate the utility of ENMs for plant promotion 
and protection. Nanotechnology applications in the food industry involve encap-
sulation and delivery of materials to targeted sites, developing flavor, introducing 
nano-antimicrobial agents in food, improving shelf life, sensing contamination, 
improving food preservatives, monitoring, tracing, and logo protection. The list of 
environmental problems that the world faces is very long, but the strategies to fix 
these problems are relatively few. Scientists from all over the world are develop-
ing nanomaterials that could use selected nanomaterials to capture poisonous pollut-
ants from water and degrade solid waste into useful products. The market intake of 
nanomaterials is increasing, and the Freedonia Group has predicted that the market 
for nanostructures will grow to $100 billion by 2025. Nanotechnology research and 
development has been steadily increasing across all scientific disciplines and indus-
tries. Based on this background, the scientific series entitled “Nanobiotechnology for 
Plant Protection” was inspired by the desire of the editor, Kamel A. Abd-Elsalam, 
to put together detailed, up-to-date, and applicable studies on nanobiotechnology 
applications in the field of agro-ecosystems to foster awareness and extend our view 
of future perspectives.
The main appeal of this book series is its specific focus on plant protection in 
agri-food and environment, which is one of the most topical issues of the many chal-
lenges faced by humanity today. The discovery and highlighting of new book in-
puts, based on nanobiotechnology, that can be used at lower application rates will be 
critical to the sustainability of eco-agriculture. The research carried out in the related 
fields is scattered and not in a single place. This book series will cover applications 
in the agri-food and environment sectors, which is the new topic of research in the 
field of nanobiotechnology. This book series will present a comprehensive account 
of the literature on specific nanomaterials and their applications in agriculture, food, 
and environment. Readers will be able to gather information from a single book 
series. Students, teachers, researchers from colleges, universities, research institutes, 
and industry will benefit from this book series. Four specific features make this book 
series one of a kind. First, this book series has a very specific editorial focus, and re-
searchers can locate nanotechnology information precisely without looking through 
the entire text. Second, and more importantly, this series offers a crucial evaluation of 
the content material along with nanomaterials, technologies, applications, methods 
and equipment, and safety and regulatory aspects in agri-food and environmental sci-
ences. Third, this series offers readers a concise description of the content material; 
it offers nanoscientists clarity and in-depth information. Finally, this series presents 
researchers with insights on new discoveries. The current series gives researchers 
a sense of what to do, what they would need to do, and how to do it properly—by

finding others who have done it. The fifth book in this series entitled Agri-Waste and 
Microbes for Production of Sustainable Nanomaterials has gathered the know-how, 
mechanisms, and green production of various nanomaterials by using agri-waste and 
microorganisms. The expected readership for this book series would be researchers 
in the field of environmental science, food, and agriculture science. Some readers 
may also include chemists, material scientists, government regulatory agencies, agro 
and food industry players, and academicians. Readers from industry may also be 
interested in this series. This book series will be useful to a wide audience of food, 
agriculture, and environmental science researchers, including undergraduate and 
graduate students and postgraduates. In addition, agricultural producers could bene-
fit from the applied knowledge that will be highlighted in this book, which otherwise 
would be buried in different journals. Both primary and secondary audiences seek 
up-to-date knowledge of nanotechnology applications in environmental science, ag-
riculture, and food science. It is a trending area, and many new studies are published 
every week. Readers need some good summaries to help them learn the latest key 
findings, which could be review articles and/or books. This book series will help to 
put these pockets of knowledge together and make it more easily accessible globally.

1  ­Introduction
Research in nanotechnology is primarily concerned with the synthesis of nanoparticles 
of various size and chemical compositions. The monodispersity of the nanoparticles 
being synthesized also occupies a significant place when application part is consid-
ered. This is ensured by providing control over the different parameters involved. 
Further, the need for a procedure to synthesize nanoparticles in an eco-friendly pro-
cess has increased leading to focus on green synthetic methods (Kamel et al., 2016).
Nanoparticles are being synthesized by physical, chemical as well as biologi-
cal methods. Ball milling, freeze-drying, spread drying, precipitation, ion sputtering 
and mechanical smashing are some of the physical methods involved. Reduction 
by photocatalytic, electrochemical, chemical, biochemical or irradiation methods 
is the most commonly employed chemical processes. Micro-emulsion method and 
microwave-assisted techniques are also used to synthesize nanoparticles (Singh, 
2016). The physical method of nanoparticle synthesis relies on the top-down ap-
proach which involves the breakdown of larger substances to form nano-sized par-
ticles. This needs a lot of energy to provide high pressure and high temperature for 
the reactions to take place. Apart from being a costly technique, this also generates 
several toxic compounds. Biogenic methods, on the other hand, follow the pattern of 
bottom-up approach where atoms or molecules are assembled to form a nanoparticle 
(Edison and Pradeep, 2020; Golinska et al., 2014). This is a self-limiting process 
where the assembly occurs under a controlled condition. The physical and chemical 
methods of nanoparticle synthesis are eventually being replaced by biological meth-
ods due to their eco-friendly and environmentally sustainable approaches. The fact 
that green synthesis of nanoparticles utilizes a comparatively less amount of toxic 
chemicals and provides a safe environment has gained them a greater interest among 
Actinomycetes-assisted 
nanoparticles: Synthesis 
and applications

376
CHAPTER 16  Actinomycetes-assisted nanoparticles
researchers worldwide. Moreover, this results in the production of a large number of 
nanoparticles within a short time (Deljou and Goudarzi, 2016).
Biosynthesis of nanoparticles is carried out with the help of plants and microor-
ganisms which act as excellent capping and stabilizing agents. Plant-derived phy-
tochemicals and enzymes are utilized for the reduction of metallic substances to 
nanoparticles. Similarly, microorganisms have the inherent capacity to accumulate 
or adsorb metals which makes them good candidates for many biotechnological ap-
proaches. The application of microorganisms ensures a simple, non-toxic and envi-
ronmentally benign method of nanoparticle synthesis. This is one of the promising 
areas of research with greater scope and applications. Microorganisms, both unicel-
lular and multicellular are exploited to synthesize different types, shapes and sizes 
of nanoparticles under controlled conditions. They act as biofactories, which syn-
thesize nanomaterials either extra-cellularly or store them intra-cellularly in specific 
compartments. This property of microorganisms makes them desirable candidates in 
nanoparticle synthesis and proves to be efficient and cost-effective under in vitro con-
ditions. Nanoparticles such as gold, silver, cadmium, zinc, magnesium etc. are being 
synthesized through microbial methods (Kumar et al., 2015). Engineered nanomate-
rials find their applications in the manufacture of smart fertilizers and encapsulated 
growth hormones. They are also employed in bioremediation processes (McShane 
and Sunahara, 2015). Nanoparticles have numerous applications in sectors such as 
agriculture, food, environmental management, automobiles, health, pharmaceuticals, 
electronics and appliances (Fig. 1).

1  ­Introduction
Actinomycetes refer to a group of heterotrophic, gram-positive, non-motile, soil 
bacteria which resemble the fungi morphologically (Franco-Correa and Chavarro-
Anzola, 2016). They are ubiquitous and are placed under the order Actinomycetales. 
They are the candidates responsible for the characteristic earthy aroma one can feel 
immediately after the first shower. Geosmin, a chemical compound produced by ac-
tinomycetes, along with the oils secreted by specific plants after a dry season is 
responsible for the earthy scent, called petrichor. They are also called as ray fungi 
(Strahlenpilze), thread or ray bacteria, due to the presence of branched, filamentous 
structures resembling fungal hyphae, the difference being their length. These hyphae 
are much smaller (1–2μm diameter) than fungal hyphae (5–10μm) which distinguish 
them from fungi. Most of the actinomycetes are aerobic but some of them can be 
anaerobic too.
The actinomycetes are classified into 7 families based on hyphal and repro-
ductive structures: Streptomycetaceae (e.g., Streptomyces, Microdlobaspone and 
Sporictilhya), Nocardiaceae (e.g., Nocardia, Pseudonocardia), Micromononsporaceae 
(e.g., Micromonospora, Thermonospora, Actinobifida, Thermoactinomycetes), 
Actinoplanaceae (e.g., Streptosporangium, Actinoplanes, Plasmobispora and 
Dactylosporangium), Dermatophilaceae (e.g., Geodermatophilus), Frankiaceae 
(e.g., Frankia) and Actinomycetaceae (e.g., Actinomyces). More than 500 species 
of actinomycetes have been described so far. Among these Streptomyces is a well-
known genus due to their industrially important products. About 80% of the antibiot-
ics are produced using these organisms (Silva-Vinhote et al., 2017).
The actinomycetes are usually harmless except a few which are pathogenic to hu-
mans and animals. They can either live in marine or terrestrial habitats, especially on 
decomposable organic substances. The actinomycetes in soil act as saprophytes with 
chemo-organotrophic nature. They can decompose recalcitrant substances and also 
complex molecules such as chitin, hemicellulose and cellulose. The actinomycetes 
include high pH and temperature tolerant organisms which makes them industrially 
important.
Actinomycetes produce economically valuable products like vitamins, enzymes, 
fungicides, insecticides and herbicides. These products are usually eco-friendly. 
Despite being highly effective agents, they pronounce less toxic to non-target organ-
isms and this distinguishes them from many commercially available synthetic prod-
ucts. According to Balagurunathan and Radhakrishnan (2010) actinomycete derived 
products can be listed under five heads:
	(i)	Primary metabolites such as enzymes, vitamins, aminoacids, nucleotides, 
carotenoids and siderophores.
	(ii)	Secondary metabolites like antimicrobial agents, insecticides, anticancer 
agents, pigments, herbicides, immunosuppressants and growth promoters.
	(iii)	Products of bioconversion include tetracycline, chitosan, pentachloraniline and 
phenyl acetic acid.
	(iv)	Actinomycete cells are used as biofertilizers, biopesticides and single-cell 
proteins.
	(v)	Recombinant products

378
CHAPTER 16  Actinomycetes-assisted nanoparticles
Secondary metabolites and bioactive compounds produced by actinomycetes are 
of industrial importance. Actinomycetes find applications in agro-industry too due to 
their ability to act as biocontrol agents. Immunosuppressants and chemotherapeutic 
agents are synthesized employing actinomycetes. Halophilic actinomycetes are used 
in the synthesis of metabolites and enzymes of industrial importance. With the ad-
vent of science and technology, these organisms have found their applications in the 
synthesis of inorganic/heavy metal nanoparticles. Studies suggest that the actinomy-
cete Frankia is involved in biological nitrogen fixation by inducing nodule formation 
in the roots of actinorhizal plants. The present chapter summarizes the synthesis, 
characterization and applications of different nanoparticles synthesized using actino-
mycetes as stabilizing and capping agents.
2  ­Isolation of actinomycetes
Actinomycetes are usually isolated from soil but marine sediments and water are also 
good sources of actinomycetes. Marine actinomycetes have been proved to be good 
sources of antibiotics and are also explored for their ability to synthesize nanoparticles. 
Kumar and Jadeja (2016) have discussed some of the genera such as Rhodococcus, 
Streptosporangium, Nocardia, Pseudonocardia, Micromonospora, Marinospora, 
Streptomyces, Salinospora, Aeromicrobium, Verrucosispora, Lamerjespora and 
Williamsia isolated from marine samples. These organisms demand pretreatments, en-
richment and specialized technique for isolation. Plants and insects are also identified 
to be sources of actinomycetes. Endophytic actinomycetes are those organisms which 
remain associated with plants and are recently being explored for identification of novel 
metabolites. Actinomycetes have been isolated from the gut of termites using a defat-
ted wood powder based medium. Lignin-related media and cellulose-related media are 
also good sources for the growth of actinomycetes in culture but these media allow 
growth of selected organisms alone (Watanabe et al., 2003). Fatmawati et al. (2018) 
have isolated Streptomyces sp. from the rhizospheric soil of maize using Humic-Acid 
Vitamin Agar (HVA) medium. Different methods are employed by researchers to iso-
late actinomycetes, which depends mostly on the source, organism and purpose of the 
study. The method of isolating actinomycetes from the soil is given in Fig. 2.
3  ­Actinomycete assisted synthesis of nanoparticles
Green nano actinobacteriology is an interface between actinobacteriology and 
nanotechnology. As the name implies, this is a novel field of nanotechnology 
concerned with the synthesis of nanoparticles employing actinomycetes, its char-
acterization and applications (Singh, 2016). Actinomycetes are promising candi-
dates in the field of nanotechnology. They act as excellent reducing and stabilizing 
agents in the green synthesis of nanoparticles. This is brought about by the action 
of microbial enzymes and proteins which as capping agents in the biosynthesis 
make the nanoparticles well dispersed (Golinska et al., 2014). The actinomycetes 

379
3  ­Actinomycete assisted synthesis of nanoparticles
are capable of producing highly stable, monodispersed as well as polydispersed 
nanoparticles. Actinomycete assisted methods of synthesis to provide a large sur-
face to mass ratio, high sensitivity, ultra-small size with less toxicity and all these 
properties make them more desirable in therapeutic, pharmaceutical and other in-
dustrial applications (Manimaran and Kannabiran, 2017). Actinomycetes also offer 
ample opportunities for research in nanobiotechnology and these organisms can 
be genetically manipulated to allow maximum productivity of nanoparticles with 
desired composition and size.
The synthesis of nanoparticles employing microorganisms usually follows the 
bottom-up method in which smaller entities are assembled to form nano-sized par-
ticles. Here the atoms unite to form nuclei which in turn give rise to the nanoparticle. 
Microbial enzymes catalyze the synthesis of nanostructures under the influence of 
pH, temperature and other reaction conditions (Keat et al., 2015).
Nanoparticle synthesis using actinomycetes involves the following steps:
•	
Isolation of actinomycetes from soil
•	
Treatment of metal solution to synthesize nanoparticle
•	
Characterization of nanoparticles

mycetes, upon exposure to metal ions release specific enzymes which interact with

the metals either extracellularly or intracellularly after transporting the metals into 
the cell with the help of proteins. These metals are reduced to yield nanoparticles of 
varying dimensions and structures. Nanoparticle synthesis is usually governed by 
the control established over these processes. Selection of the initial seed followed 
by a delicate control over the external factors such as pH, temperature and other ki-
netic energy barriers becomes a crucial part which decides the intrinsic shape of the 
nanoparticle (Liu et al., 2013).
3.1  ­Extracellular vs intracellular synthesis
Reduction of the metal ions by the cell wall components leads to extracellular synthesis 
of nanoparticles by microorganisms. Proteins, enzymes or other organic compounds 
present in media can also lead to extracellular production (Fang et al., 2019). In acti-
nomycetes, the extracellular synthesis of nanoparticles occurs due to the presence of 
enzymes related to the nitrogen cycle. These enzymes reduce the metal ions through 
electron shuttle process (Aswani et al., 2019; Manimaran and Kannabiran, 2017).
The extracellular synthesis of nanoparticles is generally considered to be eco-
nomically important due to the ease of production (Deljou and Goudarzi, 2016). 
It has been noticed that the extracellular synthesis of nanoparticles minimizes the 
downstream processing.
A halotolerant actinomycete, Rhodococcus sp. has been used for the extracellular 
synthesis of monodisperse gold nanoparticles by reduction of AuCl4. Another acti-
nomycete Thermomonospora sp. which is an alkalo-thermophilic organism has also 
been employed for the extracellular production of monodisperse gold nanoparticles. 
The nanoparticles are found on the cytoplasmic membrane as well as on the cell wall 
(Ahmad et al., 2003a,b; Sehgal et al., 2018).
Intracellular synthesis, on the other hand, involves the transport of metal ions into 
the microbial cells where nanoparticles are produced under the influence of enzymes. 
Electrostatic interaction occurs between the metal ions and carboxyl groups present in 
the cell wall of microorganisms leading to their entry into the cell. Due to an interac-
tion between these metal ions and intracellular proteins in the presence of co-factors 
the corresponding nanoparticles are formed (Li et al., 2011; Fang et al., 2019; Golinska 
et al., 2014). Whenever there is an increase in the pH, there arises a high competi-
tion for the negatively charged binding sites between the metal ions and protons. The 
synthesis of silver nanoparticles is an example wherein an electrostatic interaction be-
tween the positively charged silver ions and negatively charged carboxylate groups of 
enzymes located in the cell wall can be witnessed. The Ag + ions get trapped on cell 
surface, interact with these enzymes, form silver nuclei as a result of reduction and 
finally lead to the formation of silver nanoparticles (Abdeen et al., 2014).
Rhodococcus sp. can synthesize gold nanoparticles by extracellular as well as in-
tracellular modes. The nanoparticles are synthesized on the cytoplasmic ­membrane of 
this organism (Sehgal et al., 2018). When compared to the extracellular synthesis, the 
intracellularly synthesized nanoparticles need additional procedures such as deter-
gent treatment or ultrasonication to release the product out of the cell. Actinomycetes 
are capable of synthesizing nanoparticles of varying shapes and size (Table 1).

CHAPTER 16  Actinomycetes-assisted nanoparticles
3.1.1  ­Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs)
Silver ions are toxic to several microorganisms, a property which has been of focus 
in the development of antibiotics employing silver compounds. Silver nanoparticles 
are one of the most widely employed nanomaterials (Kumar et al., 2015). They have 
a large surface to volume ratio that makes them effective against many bacteria. 
A few strains of bacteria show resistance against silver and these are the preferred 
candidates for silver nanoparticle synthesis. During the synthesis of nanoparticles, 
silver ions are reduced to form silver nuclei in the presence of a α-NADH-dependent 
nitrase reductase enzyme (Manimaran and Kannabiran, 2017; Golinska et al., 2014). 
Streptomyces atrovirens, an actinomycete isolated from marine soil is capable of syn-
thesizing smooth, well dispersed, spherical shaped crystals of AgNPs with particle 
size ranging between 55 and 78nm (Subbaiya et al., 2017).
Avilala and Golla (2019) employed a mangrove derived marine actinomycete 
Nocardiopsis alba to synthesize silver nanoparticles. Spherical crystals of silver 
nanoparticles were synthesized and were found to possess antibacterial and anti-
viral activity. Spherical shaped silver nanoparticles with antibacterial properties 
were synthesized by Sadhasivam et  al. (2010) employing the extracellular com-
ponents of Streptomyces hygroscopicus. Abd-Elnaby et al. (2016) have employed 
an actinomycete, Streptomyces rochei obtained from marine sediments to synthe-
size silver nanoparticles. Spherical-shaped AgNPs of 22–85nm size was obtained. 
Another study employing Streptomyces sp. to synthesize AgNPs from AgNO3 re-
sulted in the production of nanoparticles ranging from 5 to 40nm size which had 
the therapeutic potential to act against various pathogens (Sukanya et  al., 2013). 
Silver nanoparticles of spherical shape are synthesized by numerous microorgan-
isms such as Rhodococcus sp., Streptomyces sp., S. parvulus, S. xinghaiensis OF 
1, S. rochei MHM13, Streptacidiphilus durhamensis (Otari et al., 2012; Gahlawat 
and Choudhury, 2019). Sheik et  al. (2019) have screened 17 actinomycetes and 
identified a potent strain, Streptomyces sp. DW102 which produces AgNPs with a 
broad spectrum of antibacterial activity. Streptomyces sp. NH21 has been employed 
to synthesize small-sized silver nanoparticles (44nm) in the supernatant as well as 
in the biomass (8.4nm) indicating both extracellular and intracellular production 
(Składanowski et al., 2017). Streptacidiphilus durhamensis HGG16n is an isolate of 
acidophilic actinobacteria employed by Buszewski et al. (2018) to synthesize spheri-
cal shaped AgNPs of size between 8 and 48nm.
The cell-free supernatant of an actinomycete Gordonia amicalis HS-11 has been 
utilized for the synthesis of silver nanoparticles from silver nitrate (Sowani et al., 
2016). Silver nanoparticles have been synthesized with the aid of three Streptomyces 
sp. among which Streptomyces parvulus DPUA 1549 strain tested to be more efficient 
than the other two strains- Streptomyces seoulensis DPUA 1747 and Streptomyces 
owasiensis DPUA 1748 (Silva-Vinhote et al., 2017).
3.1.2  ­Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)
Gold nanoparticles of varying size and shape can be synthesized by controlling 
the growth parameters such as pH and temperature (Gericke and Pinches, 2006). 

383
3  ­Actinomycete assisted synthesis of nanoparticles
Streptomyces griseoruber is used in the green synthesis of 5–50nm sized gold 
nanoparticles. Spherical, hexagonal or triangular nanoparticles are synthesized by 
S. griseoruber (Ranjitha and Rai, 2017). Similarly, Bennur et al. (2016) has em-
ployed the isolates of an actinomycete Gordonia amarae to produce gold nanopar-
ticles of spherical, polycrystalline nature. Similar work carried out by Sowani et al. 
(2016) using Gordonia amicalis HS-11 strain resulted in glycolipid mediated syn-
thesis of gold nanoparticles. AuNPs of particle size 19–28nm has been synthesized 
employing Streptomyces griseus isolated from the sediments of Suez Gulf, Egypt 
(Hamed and Abdelftah, 2019). A study conducted by Składanowski et al. (2017) 
used Streptomyces sp. NH21 isolated from humic soil of the acidic pine forests in 
Poland. This strain was capable of synthesizing spherical shaped gold nanoparticles 
of 10nm size. Ten strains of Streptomycetes isolated from the Himalayas were em-
ployed in the synthesis of gold nanoparticles from chloroauric acid. Among these, 
the strain Streptomyces viridogens HM10 gave promising results by producing 
spherical and rod-shaped gold nanoparticles of average particle size between 18 and 
20nm (Balagurunathan et al., 2011). Thermomonospora sp. and Rhodococcus sp. 
have been reported to synthesize gold nanoparticles (Ahmad et al., 2003a,b). Dehnad 
et al. (2015) has studied the ability of Arthrobacter nitroguajacolicus isolated from 
gold mine to synthesize gold nanoparticles. Bennur et al. (2020) have synthesized 
and characterized AuNPs using an actinomycete, Nocardiopsis dassonvillei NCIM 
5124. Spherical shaped gold nanoparticles of size ranging between 10 and 25nm 
were obtained.
3.1.3  ­Copper nanoparticles (CuNPs)
Rasool and Hemalatha (2017) employed a seaweed isolated marine endophytic actino-
mycetes to synthesize copper nanoparticles. It was found that these nanoparticles were 
capable of controlling pathogens that cause diseases in humans. Streptomyces griseo-
ruber, an actinomycete isolated from is capable of synthesizing copper nanoparticles. 
Streptomyces capillispiralis Ca-1 strain of actinomycetes isolated from the medicinal 
plant Convulvulus arvensis forms spherical monodispersed copper nanoparticles of 
particle size 3.6–59nm (Hassan et al., 2018). Copper oxide nanoparticles have been 
synthesized using actinomycetes isolated from soil samples and has been proved to 
have remarkable activities against human and fish pathogens (Nabila and Kannabiran, 
2018). Similarly crystalline, spherical shaped copper oxide nanoparticles of size 
78nm and 80nm were synthesized employing two strains of endophytic actinomycetes 
(Streptomyces zaomyceticus Oc-5 and Streptomyces pseudogriseolus Acv-11) isolated 
from the leaves of Oxalis corniculata L. (Hassan et al., 2019).
3.1.4  ­Selenium nanoparticles (SeNPs)
Extracellular synthesis of selenium nanoparticles of size ranging between 100 
and 250nm has been observed while employing Streptomyces griseoruber. These 
nanoparticles have shown notable cytotoxicity against HT-29 cell lines suggesting 
their application as chemotherapeutic agents (Ranjitha and Ravishankar, 2018). 
Ameri et  al. (2016) have synthesized selenium nanoparticles using Streptomyces 

384
CHAPTER 16  Actinomycetes-assisted nanoparticles
griseobrunneus strain FSHH12. Ratnakomala et al. (2018) have screened 98 iso-
lates of actinomycetes out of which two isolates were capable of producing selenium 
nanoparticles of less than 100nm size by reduction of Se4 + ions in SeO2 to SeNPs. 
Another actinomycetes, Streptomyces microflavus strain FSHJ31 has been observed 
to produce selenium nanoparticles of 48nm size (Forootanfar et al., 2014).
3.1.5  ­Other metals
Silica nanoparticles have been produced by reduction of magnesium trisilicate hy-
drate using actinomycetes isolated from a polluted site (Mohanraj et  al., 2020). 
Mollaei et al. (2019) used cell lysis supernatant of actinomycetes isolated from the 
Persian Gulf to synthesize platinum nanoparticles, which resulted in the formation 
of 36nm crystals.
3.2  ­Characterization of nanoparticles
Depending on the initiator substances, genera (and even the strain!) of actino-
mycete used and reaction conditions are chosen, nanoparticles attain a variety of 
shapes and sizes. Spherical, triangular, hexagonal, square and rod-shaped nanopar-
ticles are the ones which are observed frequently. The characterization of nanopar-
ticles calls for detection at very low concentrations and however, this cannot be 
performed using a single methodology. UV-vis, SEM, TEM, FTIR, XRD, EDX, 
DLS and AFM are some of the techniques used to analyze the physico-chemical 
properties of nanoparticles.
3.2.1  ­UV-vis spectroscopy (UV-vis)
UV-vis spectroscopy is one of the most commonly employed techniques to examine 
the formation of nanoparticles. This is a study concerned with the optical proper-
ties of materials, which helps in application of nanoparticles for different purposes 
(Abbas, 2019). This technique records the absorption spectrum of the medium con-
taining the synthesized nanoparticles to determine the presence or absence of NPs. 
Wavelengths in the range of 200–800nm are used to study the metallic nanoparticles. 
Silver and gold nanoparticles are detected at a wavelength between 400 and 450nm 
and 500–550nm respectively (Singh, 2016).
3.2.2  ­Transmission electron microscopy (TEM)
Transmission electron microscopic analysis is employed to determine the structure, 
size and composition of the nanoparticle. Grain size, morphology and size distri-
bution of actinomycete synthesized nanoparticles can be analyzed using this tech-
nique. The size and shape of nanoparticles get 1000 times higher resolution than 
SEM analysis, which makes this imaging noteworthy. The major drawback of TEM 
analysis is the longer sample preparation time and a narrow range of sample selection 
required. High-resolution-transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) analysis is 
an advanced tool for better resolution of the crystallographic images of materials 
down to Angstrom level (Zhou and Thompson, 2017; Bar et al., 2009).

385
3  ­Actinomycete assisted synthesis of nanoparticles
3.2.3  ­Scanning electron microscopy (SEM)
Morphology, surface topography and distribution of nanoparticles can be studied 
with the help of a scanning electron microscope. Advancement in this technique sup-
ports the analysis of single nanoparticles of size less than 10nm. When compared to 
TEM, Scanning Electron Microscopy appears to be a statistically reliable and effi-
cient measurement tool in nanoparticle characterization. It has to be noted that TEM 
and SEM retain their uniqueness and differs in the way images are resolved. Field 
Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) is an advanced tool employed 
for high-resolution images which differ from SEM in the type of electron generation 
system used.
3.2.4  ­Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR)
FTIR helps in identification of the functional groups present in the nanoparticle by 
producing an IR absorption spectrum. A distinct molecular profile is produced for 
each sample which is visualized as a unique fingerprint. This allows the detection of 
the compound as well as the chemical composition of the synthesized nanoparticle 
using infra-red radiation between 4000 and 400cm− 1. Attenuated total reflectance-
Fourier transmission infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) analysis is also employed to 
characterize the functional groups of nanoparticles.
3.2.5  ­X-ray diffraction (XRD)
X-ray diffraction is a non-destructive method used to determine the mean size as 
well as the crystalline nature of the nanoparticles. As the wavelength of X-rays is in 
the atomic scale, this technique becomes an important tool to analyze nanomaterials. 
Information such as phase identification, quantification, crystal structure variations, 
crystallinity, orientation, size and shape of crystallite can be obtained using XRD.
3.2.6  ­Energy dispersive X-ray diffraction (EDX/EDAX/EDS)
This is a method that helps to determine the elemental composition of the synthe-
sized NPs. This technique is based on the analysis of elements present in the sample 
by a generation of X-rays. It provides semi-qualitative as well as semi-quantitative 
information of the sample. They can be attached to SEM or TEM to identify the de-
sired specimen (Scimeca et al., 2018).
3.2.7  ­Dynamic light scattering (DLS)
DLS, also called as Quasi Elastic Light Scattering (QELS) is a non-invasive tech-
nique, used to measure the surface charge and size distribution of synthesized 
nanoparticles which are kept suspended in a liquid (Manivasagan et al., 2016). They 
are much useful in studying sub-micron sized particles and so nanoparticles can be 
best studied using this method.
3.2.8  ­Atomic force microscopy (AFM)
AFM analysis is used to determine the shape and particle size of nanomaterials. This 
involves sonication of the sample followed by centrifugation to extract the pellet, 

386
CHAPTER 16  Actinomycetes-assisted nanoparticles
which harbors the nanoparticles. The pellet is prepared as a thin film over a glass 
slide and analyzed under a microscope (Majeed et al., 2016).
3.2.9  ­Other techniques
Parameters like size, shape, size distribution, growth kinetics, concentration, struc-
tural defects, elemental composition, chemical composition, crystal structure, density, 
agglomeration state, properties of a single particle, optical and magnetic properties 
are some characteristics being analyzed in a nanoparticle. Techniques such as BET, 
XAS, SAXS, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Nuclear Magnetic Resonance 
Spectroscopy (NMR), TGA, LEIS, PL spectroscopy, NTA, DCS, ICP-MS, ICP-OES, 
Mossbauer, FMR, XMCD, VSM, MFM, SQUID, RMM-MEMS, SIMS, MALDI, 
Time-of-Flight Secondary Ion Mass Spectrometry (ToF-SIMS), EELS, and ESBD 
are used in characterization of nanoparticles (Mourdikoudis et al., 2018).
4  ­Applications of actinomycete synthesized nanoparticles
4.1  ­Agriculture
Actinomycete synthesized nanoparticles find their applications as nano-­fertilizers. 
They are also used as biocontrol agents in sustainable agricultural practices. 
Nanoparticles have been used in the form of nanofertilizers, nanoherbicides, 
nanopesticides, etc. They are also employed in smart delivery systems which allow 
the release of agrochemicals in a controlled manner. For example, pesticides are 
encapsulated in nanomaterials and applied to soil. This prevents the release of bulk 
chemicals that cause toxicity to crops and also ensures the pesticide availability for 
longer periods (Mishra et al., 2017).
4.2  ­Catalytic activity (nanocatalyst)
Gold nanoparticles synthesized using the actinomycete Streptomyces griseoruber 
acts as a nanocatalyst in degradation of the textile dye, methylene blue. The reduc-
tion of Methylene Blue by NaBH4 is enhanced in the presence of this nanoparticle 
(Ranjitha and Rai, 2017).
4.3  ­Antimicrobial activity
Antimicrobial activity of silver, gold, copper and zinc nanoparticles have been dem-
onstrated by some scientists across the world. When compared to chemically synthe-
sized nanoparticles, the biogenic nanoparticles show higher antimicrobial activity. 
This property of green synthesized nanoparticles can be attributed to the influential 
action of proteins which act as capping agents (Kora and Rastogi, 2013). One of the 
promising candidates of the actinomycete family is the Streptomyces sp. which is 
involved in the production of antibiotics. They are capable of producing a large num-
ber of secondary metabolites (Ahmed et al., 2020). Silver nanoparticles synthesized 

387
4  ­Applications of actinomycete synthesized nanoparticles
using Streptomyces hygroscopicus were effective against bacteria such as Bacillus 
subtilis, Enterococcus faecalis, Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhimurium and the 
pathogenic yeast, Candida albicans (Sadhasivam et al., 2010). Multidrug-resistant 
organisms such as Escherichia coli, Enterococcus faecalis, Klebsiella pneumonia 
and Staphylococcus aureus were controlled by the application of AgNPs synthesized 
using the potent strain Streptomyces sp. DW102 (Sheik et al., 2019).
Silver nanoparticles biosynthesized employing a marine actinomycete 
Streptomyces rochei exhibited a wide range of inhibition against several bacteria 
such as Bacillus cereus, Bacillus subtilis, Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aerugi-
nosa, Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella typhimurium, Vibrio damsela and Vibrio 
fluvialis (Abd-Elnaby et al., 2016). Sukanya et al. (2013) have shown that AgNPs 
synthesized using Streptomyces sp. inhibits the pathogenic microorganisms such as 
Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Klebsiella pneumonia and Staphylococcus aureus. Copper 
nanoparticles synthesized using Streptomyces capillispiralis Ca-1 inhibits Alternaria 
spp., Fusarium spp., Aspergillus niger and Pythium spp. (Hassan et  al., 2018). 
Gold nanoparticles synthesized employing the marine actinomycete, Streptomyces 
griseus showed antimicrobial activity against Escherichia coli, Enterococcus fae-
calis, Salmonella typhimurium, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Vibrio damsel, Vibrio 
fluvialis and the yeast Candida albicans (Hamed and Abdelftah, 2019). Silver 
nanoparticles obtained from the starved cultures of Streptomyces sp. NH21 showed 
significant antibacterial activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Bacillus sub-
tilis (Składanowski et al., 2017). It has been observed that copper oxide nanoparticles 
can inhibit the growth of pathogenic bacteria such as Aeromonas caviae, Aeromonas 
hydrophila, Bacillus cereus, Edwardsiella tarda, Proteus mirabilis, Staphylococcus 
aureus and Vibrio anguillarum (Nabila and Kannabiran, 2018). Hassan et al. (2019) 
have shown that the copper oxide nanoparticles synthesized using Streptomyces zao-
myceticus Oc-5 and Streptomyces pseudogriseolus Acv-11 have a wide spectrum of 
antimicrobial activity. They have observed promising results against different or-
ganisms such as Gram positive bacteria (Bacillus subtilis, Staphylococcus aureus 
and Bacillus diminuta), Gram negative bacteria (Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas 
aeruginosa), unicellular fungi (Candida albican), multicellular fungi (Aspergillus 
brasiliensis) and phyto-pathogenic fungi (Fusarium oxysporum, Pythium ultimum, 
Aspergillus niger and Alternaria alternata). Gold nanoparticles synthesized using 
Streptomyces viridogens has a good antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus 
aureus and Escherichia coli (Balagurunathan et al., 2011). Silver nanoparticles pro-
duced using the endophytic actinomycete, Streptomyces sp. isolated from Mentha 
longifolia (L.) showed antimicrobial activity against the fungi Candida albicans and 
Aspergillus brasiliensis. Bacteria such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Escherichia 
coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Bacillus dimenuta were also inhibited by this nanopar-
ticle (El-Gamal et al., 2018). Bacillus subtilis, Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus au-
reus and Micrococcus luteus were inhibited by selenium nanoparticles produced by 
actinomycetes isolated from Lombok Island soil samples (Ratnakomala et al., 2018). 
The antimicrobial activity of actinomycetes synthesized nanoparticles is listed in

4  ­Applications of actinomycete synthesized nanoparticles
4.4  ­Anti-oxidant properties
Hassan et al. (2019) have reported high radical scavenging activity and total anti-
oxidant activity for the copper oxide nanoparticles synthesized using Streptomyces 
pseudogriseolus Acv-11 when compared to that of Streptomyces zaomyceticus Oc-5. 
Gold and silver nanoparticles obtained using a soil actinomycetes, Gordonia amica-
lis showed free radical scavenging activities (Sowani et al., 2016).
4.5  ­Anti-malarial and anti-parasitic activity
Gold nanoparticles derived from a marine actinomycete has been proved to pos-
sess antimalarial activity (Karthik et al., 2013). Streptomyces sp. LK3 synthesized 
silver nanoparticles were found to be effective acaricides against the parasites 
Rhipicephalus microplus and Haemaphysalis bispinosa (Karthik et al., 2014).
4.6  ­Dye degradation
Selenium crystals obtained by biogenic synthesis using Streptomyces griseobrun-
neus FSHH12 resulted in photocatalytic degradation of bromothymol blue (Ameri 
et al., 2016). Silica nanoparticles have been immobilized by encapsulation to treat 
textile effluent and this resulted in 80% decolorization (Mohanraj et al., 2020).
4.7  ­Anti-biofouling activity
Algae, bacteria, protozoa and barnacles grow and gradually accumulate over-­
engineering materials such as pipes, underwater equipment etc., causing biofouling. 
Biofilm formation is the major cause behind this and needs to be eliminated to ensure 
a trouble-free operation of the equipment. Biofilm formation can be prevented by the 
application of silver nanoparticles. These NPs disrupt the mechanism of biofilm for-
mation and also inhibit the growth of biofouling bacteria (Manivasagan et al., 2016).
4.8  ­Larvicidal activity
Copper nanoparticles synthesized using Streptomyces zaomyceticus Oc-5 and 
Streptomyces pseudogriseolus Acv-11 strains of actinomycetes show good larvicidal 
activities against Musca domestica and Culex pipiens (Hassan et al., 2019).
4.9  ­Cytotoxicity and anticancer activity
Silver nanoparticles synthesized using Streptomyces atrovirens showed excellent cy-
totoxic effects on MCF-7 human breast cancer cell lines, which suggests that these 
nanoparticles can be used for therapeutic purposes as nanomedicine in the treatment 
of human breast cancer (Subbaiya et al., 2017). Hamed and Abdelftah (2019) studied 
the anticancer activity of gold nanoparticles synthesized using Streptomyces griseus 
against MCF-7, a breast cancer cell line and HCT-116, a colon cancer cell line which 

390
CHAPTER 16  Actinomycetes-assisted nanoparticles
gave promising results. Hassan et al. (2019) employed Streptomyces zaomyceticus 
Oc-5 and Streptomyces pseudogriseolus Acv-11 strains to produce copper nanopar-
ticles which showed cytotoxic effects on Vero (normal cell line obtained from kidney 
of African green monkey) and Caco-2 (human colorectal adenocarcinoma cells) cell 
lines demonstrating their application in cancer therapeutics. An endophytic actino-
mycete Streptomyces antimycoticus L-1 was used for the green synthesis of silver 
nanoparticles which was efficient against Caco-2 cancer cell lines (Salem et al., 2020).
4.10  ­Other applications
Pyrrolo compounds derived from marine actinomycetes (LG003 and LG005) have 
been used for the synthesis of AgO and Ag2O3 nanoparticles. These nanoparticles 
have shown good anti-dermatophytic activity and this makes it a promising candidate 
for anti-fungal agents (Ravi et al., 2020). Nano finished fabrics coated with silver 
nanoparticles synthesized employing Streptomyces antimycoticus L-1 have demon-
strated antibacterial activity even after 10 washing cycles (Salem et al., 2020). A 
novel actinomycete Nocardiopsis dassonvillei- DS013 isolated from marine sedi-
ments has been used for silver nanoparticle synthesis and these NPs showed bacteri-
cidal effects on clinical isolates (Dhanaraj et al., 2020).
5  ­Toxicity of nanoparticles
The nanoparticles synthesized using actinomycetes find enormous applications. But 
the fact that these nanoparticles can pose environmental, as well as health hazards, 
cannot be denied. The nanomaterials tend to accumulate in plants and animals lead-
ing to biomagnification. Soil fertility is lost due to the contamination of soil with 
nanoparticles and they can also interact with environmental factors (McShane and 
Sunahara, 2015). Nanoparticles due to their extremely small size can enter into the 
human body through food, inhalation or even through skin. Under such cases, they 
lead to a number of health hazards such as inflammation, decrease in glutathione 
levels, oxidative stress, production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) leading to en-
hanced apoptosis, etc. (Golinska et al., 2014).
Highly sensitive combustible nano-powders have been developed which, due to 
their coagulative properties can explode if not handled with care. This poses a huge 
risk to the users and therefore proper preventive and precautionary steps have to be 
adopted while working with nanomaterials (Bouillard, 2015).
6  ­Conclusion
Actinomycetes are fungi like bacteria which have diverse applications. These or-
ganisms are capable of producing some metabolites which are of economic value. 
Nanoparticles synthesized using actinomycetes also find numerous applications in 

391
­References
agricultural as well as industrial sectors. Streptomyces sp. is one of the largest genera 
seen under actinomycetes. These organisms are capable of synthesizing nanoparticles 
with varying shapes and size. Actinomycetes produce nanoparticles by intracellular 
as well as extracellular modes. Extracellular synthesis of nanoparticles is of much 
interest due to the ease of separation and processing. During extracellular synthesis, 
the cell wall-associated enzymes reduce metal ions into nanoparticles. Intracellular 
synthesis on the contrary involves the use of enzymes and proteins present inside 
the bacterial cell, especially the nitrate reductase enzymes play an important role in 
the transformation of metals into nanoparticles. Rhodococcus, Thermomonospora, 
Streptacidiphilus, Nocardiopsis, Gordonia and Arthrobacter are few genera capable 
of synthesizing nanoparticles. These organisms produce enzymes and proteins that 
act as capping agents and also lead to a stable nanoparticle. The proteins capping 
the nanoparticles are partly responsible for their antimicrobial activity and biocon-
trol activity. The synthesis, characterization and applications of nanoparticles using 
actinomycetes are of great interest to researchers worldwide. This is because of the 
ample opportunities offered by the genera of actinomycetes to carry out research and 
the prospects of bioengineering to fabricate novel products. Further research in ac-
tinomycetes mediated nanoparticle synthesis is required to get insights into the pro-
duction systems and their pathways of NP synthesis. Filling the existing knowledge 
gaps can lead to intensive research in strain improvement, genetic engineering and 
optimization to utilize the actinomycetes for industrial production of nanoparticles.
